---
title: "Beyond Location: Airbnb Pricing Prediction using Machine Learning and Zipcode-Level Socioeconomic Features"
author: "Shiyi Li"
date: "2025-03-27"
format:
  pdf:
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt 
cite-method: citeproc
link-citations: true
suppress-bibliography: false
bibliography: references.bib # create your own bib file
csl: apa.csl
---

# Introduction

With the development of science, technology, and the global economy—as well as 
  the continued improvement in people’s quality of life—more individuals are 
  pursuing opportunities to study, work, and travel in new environments. These 
  lifestyle trends have driven a substantial increase in the demand for 
  flexible housing and accelerated the expansion of the sharing economy. 
  Airbnb, founded in 2008, exemplifies this transformation. It has grown to 
  host over 8 million active listings across more than 220 countries and has 
  accommodated over 2 billion guests and experience participants worldwide 
  [@airbnbaboutus; @moomooabnb]. By connecting hosts and guests across the 
  globe, Airbnb offers more diverse and personalized accommodation options 
  than traditional hotels, redefining short-term rentals and increasing 
  accessibility to housing choices.

One of the key reasons for Airbnb’s success is its Smart Pricing system, which 
  uses machine learning to recommend real-time rental prices based on a 
  combination of property type, location, amenities, guest reviews, and local 
  events [@clayton2024]. This dynamic pricing strategy helps align guest 
  expectations with actual property conditions and enhances both booking 
  conversion and user satisfaction. The ability to accurately predict rental 
  prices has become increasingly essential for hosts to remain competitive. 
  However, due to data limitations, this study focuses on a subset of variables 
  that are most likely to significantly affect pricing. The goal is to help 
  hosts better understand the market and develop more feasible pricing 
  strategies by analyzing patterns and relationships using interpretable and 
  predictive models.

In recent years, researchers have explored various factors influencing Airbnb 
  pricing from multiple perspectives. Prior literature has used techniques such 
  as random forests, generalized linear models (GLM), and geographically 
  weighted regression (GWR) to identify key determinants including superhost 
  status, number of amenities, review scores, property type, geographic 
  location, and review count [@kirkos2021; @zhang2017; @perezsanchez2018]. 
  These studies highlight the spatial heterogeneity of pricing factors and 
  underscore the importance of considering geographic variation in price 
  modeling.

Building upon these findings, this study utilizes a full year of Airbnb listing 
  data from New York City and integrates zipcode-level socioeconomic and 
  environmental characteristics. We compare a multivariate linear regression 
  model with an XGBoost model to assess their effectiveness in identifying and 
  predicting influential pricing factors. Unlike previous studies that tend to 
  focus on a single variable dimension, this research offers a comprehensive 
  analytical perspective across time, space, and feature dimensions. By 
  synthesizing host, listing, rating, review, geographic, and 
  macro-environmental data, we construct a more holistic modeling framework 
  that provides practical pricing guidance for Airbnb hosts and contributes 
  methodological insights for price prediction in the context of the sharing 
  economy.

# Specific Aims

Does the Airbnb listing price depend on population density? Does the Airbnb 
  listing price depend on crime rates? Does the Airbnb listing price depend on 
  income levels? Does the Airbnb listing price depend on different boroughs? 
  Which features are the most important in the XGBoost model? Which features 
  are the most important in the multivariate linear regression model? Are these 
  predictors positively or negatively associated with price? How well do a 
  multivariate linear regression model and an XGBoost model perform in 
  predicting price?

# Data

The dataset used in this project is compiled from 12 monthly Airbnb listing 
  snapshots of New York City, covering the period from April 6, 2024 to March 
  1, 2025. These datasets are sourced from Inside Airbnb [@insideAirbnbData], 
  each containing rich information about active rental listings during that 
  month. The 12 CSV files were loaded and concatenated to form a unified 
  dataset consisting of 452,951 observations and initially 79 variables. To 
  ensure data quality, we first checked for and removed any duplicate records. 
  We then verified the data types of each variable and converted them into 
  appropriate formats where necessary (e.g., converting price from string to 
  numeric, and parsing dates like host_since to datetime format). 

After examining the percentage of missing data, we removed variables with more 
  than 80% missing values, such as calendar_updated, estimated_revenue_l365d, 
  and license. The resulting cleaned dataset retained 73 features, including: 
  Host information data (host_since, host_location, host_about, 
  host_is_superhost, host_total_listings_count, etc.), Property details data 
  (property_type, room_type, accommodates, bathrooms, bedrooms, amenities, 
  price, etc.), Location and geographic data (latitude, longitude, 
  neighbourhood_cleansed, and neighbourhood_group_cleansed), Review scores data 
  (review_scores_rating, review_scores_cleanliness, review_scores_value, etc.), 
  and Availability and review history data (availability_30, number_of_reviews, 
  reviews_per_month, etc.)

Additionally, we created two new features: host_total, derived from host_since, 
  host_location, host_about, host_has_profile_pic, host_identity_verified, and 
  host_total_listings_count, representing the overall completeness and activity 
  of the host profile, and total_amenities, calculated from the amenities 
  string to quantify the number of available amenities per listing.

To supplement location-based prediction, we plan to assign zipcode values to 
  each listing using geographic coordinates, and aggregate external 
  zipcode-level data (e.g., income, crime rate, population density) from 
  external public datasets. These zipcode-based features will help capture 
  neighborhood-specific socioeconomic and environmental characteristics. This 
  dataset is particularly suitable for the research questions proposed in this 
  study, as it captures both listing-level and neighborhood-level variation in 
  property and environmental characteristics across space and time.

# Methods and Research Design

The research design begins with data preprocessing, including removing missing 
  values and aggregating zipcode-level variables such as income, crime rate, 
  and population density from public datasets. Feature engineering involves 
  constructing new features like host_total (to represent host completeness and 
  credibility) and total_amenities (to quantify listing amenities), as well as 
  generating zipcode information from geographic coordinates. I then implement 
  two modeling approaches: Multivariate Linear Regression (MLR), an 
  interpretable model that allows us to understand linear associations between 
  predictors and the outcome (price), test the significance of variables like 
  zipcode, and interpret coefficient direction, and XGBoost Regression, a 
  high-performance ensemble method that can capture non-linear patterns and 
  interactions among features, and output relative feature importance.

To assess and compare model performance, we apply 10-fold cross-validation and 
  use the following evaluation metrics: R-squared (R²), measuring how well the 
  model explains price variation, Mean Squared Error (MSE), penalizing large 
  prediction errors. To answer the statistical questions outlined in the 
  Specific Aims section, we will use the following statistical methods: using 
  simple linear regression and correlation analysis to test whether price 
  depends on population density, using multiple linear regression with 
  dummy-coded zipcodes to assess if zipcode is a statistically significant 
  predictor, using t-tests or ANOVA to compare mean prices across income and 
  crime level groups, using standardized coefficients from MLR and feature 
  importance scores from XGBoost to identify the top 5–10 significant 
  predictors, and using coefficient sign analysis to examine whether predictors 
  are positively or negatively associated with price

I will complement my analysis with the following visualization techniques: 
  Using scatter plots with regression lines to visualize price and population 
  density trends, using boxplots to show price distribution across different 
  zipcodes and income levels, using correlation heatmaps for feature 
  interdependence, using feature importance bar plots to highlight influential 
  predictors from XGBoost, using residual plots and QQ plots to evaluate 
  regression assumptions and model fit

# Discussion

This research can help Airbnb hosts better understand the factors that 
  influence pricing, providing data-driven guidance for price setting. It also 
  contributes to the broader literature on spatial pricing models and 
  predictive analytics in the sharing economy. One of the most challenging 
  aspects of this project is synthesizing target variables from existing data. 
  For instance, creating the host_total feature requires combining and 
  interpreting multiple host-related attributes such as host_since, host_about, 
  host_location, and verification indicators. Ensuring that these derived 
  variables are both meaningful and accurate adds complexity to the feature 
  engineering process. Another limitation is the absence of certain potentially 
  influential variables, such as cancellation policy, cleaning fees, or photos, 
  due to high levels of missingness. Additionally, since this study focuses 
  only on New York City, the findings may not generalize to Airbnb markets in 
  other cities or regions.

# References  

1. [Airbnb Smart Pricing] [@clayton2024]: Maximizing your Airbnb profits using
 smart pricing to stay flexible.
2. [Airbnb Company Info] [@airbnbaboutus]: About us.
3. [Airbnb on Moomoo] [@moomooabnb]: Airbnb Inc. Company Profile.
4. [Airbnb Listings’ Performance] [@kirkos2021]: determinants and predictive 
  models.
5. [Key Factors Affecting Airbnb Prices] [@zhang2017]: A geographically 
  weighted approach.
6. [Airbnb Price Determinants Study] [@perezsanchez2018]: The what, where, and 
  why of Airbnb price determinants.
7. [Inside Airbnb Data Access] [@insideAirbnbData]: The official Airbnb 
  Listings Data.
